note_en:
Unlike human beings, the debate over the question “should people live forever, if they want?” never seems to die.
 This is surprising given that the answer is obviously “yes.”In fact the arguments for “no” are so unimpressive I’m relegating them to an  Appendix.
 But what if you, like me, are curious about the inevitable next question:  “should I work on human longevity, and if so, how?"  1
 This is ultimately a personal question, and what follows won’t answer it for you (or for me, frankly).But answering it also requires understanding a shedload of strategic and economic considerations.I couldn’t find a comprehensive guide to them, so here’s a start at one.
     “We now have the understanding of biology to work on therapies that directly address the root causes of aging.”
 Is this true? And if so what’s the best way to go about it? There are (at least) three big considerations.
   Here’s a summary of what experts think causes aging in mammals and how they figured them out.
 Free radical theory of aging proposed in 1956. Strong evidence in 1990s that DNA damage leads to aging. Still not confirmed that DNA repair leads to longevity.
  Stem cells discovered 1963. Causal connection between stem cell exhaustion and aging first demonstrated in 2012.
  Replicative senescence described by Hayflick in 1965. Oncogene-induced senescence described starting in 1980s. Stress-induced senescence described starting in 1990s. Tumor suppressor loss-induced senescence described starting in 2000s. Strong evidence of therapeutic potential in 2011.
  Mitochondrial free radical theory proposed in 1972, now much less clear that it's correct. Non-ROS damage hypothesis made in 2005, evidence still accumulating.
    Discovery of telomere structure and speculation about connections to replicative senescence in 1980s. Telomere loss hypothesis for aging proposed in 1991. Experimental evidence confirming connection in 1998.
  Evidence starting to accumulate by 1983. Demonstration of causal role of chaperone system in mice in 2008. Demonstration of causal role for autophagy in mice in 2013.
      Heterochromatin loss model of aging proposed in 1997. Evidence accumulates for chromatin structure disruption at all levels in 2010s.
  Nintil’s  Longevity FAQ is a great starting point to understand the biology behind most of these proposed causes if you’re unfamiliar.
 Let’s first admit that it’s dubious to break down aging into discrete causes like in the table above. Here’s a diagram giving a much more detailed view of our current understanding of aging-related processes.Based that diagram (seriously, click the link, it’s worth it), understanding the causality of aging may be less about discovering new causes and more about disentangling what we already semi-understand, if  that’s even possible.
 Nevertheless, from the outside view the above table doesn’t really suggest that we’ve finished discovering fundamental causes of aging.The pattern — if small-N history can have patterns — seems to be that every decade a couple biological processes are associated with aging and then 1+ decades later, if ever, a causal connection is established.The table only includes causes of aging that have made their way into big general reviews, so we wouldn’t expect to, and don’t, see any with initial discovery dates past 2000.Biological processes whose heydays came after 2000 that might someday make it into this table include  microbiome changes and  RNAi dysregulation.
 From the inside view you might contradict this by accounting for increasing  investment into aging research.If investment has increased but the rate of discoveries hasn’t changed, that might mean we’re nearly done discovering things.In the same spirit you could contrast the improvement in biotech tools to the seemingly-constant rate of discoveries.
 Still, while we clearly know more about aging than we ever have, I’m hesitant to believe any claims of causal understanding.
  The  standard playbook for making a new therapeutic (the catch-all name for a drug or medical treatment) is called a  target-based approach, and goes like this:
 Identify a specific biological process that’s causing the disease you want to treat (e.g. plaque buildup, the expression of a single gene, a protein key to a virus’s function)
 Find a way to interfere with it (e.g. a synthetic molecule, an engineered antibody, a surgical intervention)  in vitro or in an animal model
  Target-based approaches work best when we have a good handle on the cause, or small number of causes, of a disease.Bacterial infections and  single-gene disorders are good examples.But as discussed above aging seems to be massively multi-causal, not to mention that we probably haven’t found all the granular causes.
  Set up an experiment where you can cheaply try a gajillion treatments and find one that affects the phenotype you care about (e.g. the phenotype of not being dead)
 In reality all therapeutic development takes place on a spectrum between target-based and phenotypic.Some understanding of causality is necessary for restricting the space of treatments screened in phenotypic approaches.And many causes of phenotypes are discovered via screening approaches.But therapeutic development today generally  leans toward target-based.
 This is despite the fact that more first-in-class small molecule drugs are  developed with phenotypic approaches.And despite the fact that many effective longevity therapies (in mice) had no mechanistic hypothesis when they were discovered, including parabiosis (and potentially  neutral blood exchange), which we still don’t understand, and caloric restriction.
 Phenotypic approaches also seem better suited to finding effective combinations of therapies, for the inelegant reason that biological systems are so interconnected that to find an effective combination therapy we’ll just have to try a lot of combinations.This is important for longevity, since it’s not clear how much partial credit we get for solving any single cause of aging.It may be that each one cured buys us more lifespan and eventually we reach  longevity escape velocity.But since dying only requires one cause of death, we may need therapies that simultaneously address all or most causes of aging — even ones we don’t know of yet — before seeing benefits.The idea that phenotypic approaches might be better for targeting multi-causal biological phenomena like aging is  not new.Companies working on them (not specifically on longevity) include  Recursion,  Janssen, and  Insitro.
 Phenotypic screening has plenty of challenges, of course.One is the gajillion factor: you need a way to try lots of different treatments, and the space of possible treatments is astronomical.Another is that you can’t do a phenotypic screen without a phenotype, and waiting for animals to die is pretty inefficient.Developing measurable phenotypes for aging, like  epigenetic clocks, is critical to enable better phenotypic screens. Gordian,  Nemalife,  Magnitude,  InVivo,  Vium, and  Spring are among the companies working on these problems.
  It seems inarguable that humans can live forever in the limit of arbitrarily fancy technology, though  some disagree.  2But whether death is conquerable not just eventually, but  with near-term tools and understanding, is not clear.To paraphrase  Lincoln: we don’t know whether we’ve sharpened the biotechnological axe enough to cut down the death tree.
 This is a key question for longevity strategy.The more confident we are that existing therapeutic technologies can make a dent in aging, the more we should invest in using them.The less confident we are, the more we need to make new tools.
 One relevant datapoint is what modalities (a.k.a. types of therapeutics) are being used by companies currently working on longevity. As far as we know, it’s about 51% small molecule drugs, 16% biologics, 18% cell therapies, and 15% gene therapies.This skews more toward non-small-molecule therapeutics than the  industry average, which is what we might expect given that most of the causes of aging in the table above don’t have obvious targets for small molecule inhibition.It’s also a marginal indication that new tools are needed, since non-small-molecule therapeutics are newer and less straightforward to develop than small-molecule drugs (as if those were straightforward to develop in the first place).
 Another datapoint is what we’ve managed to accomplish so far in mice.Here’s a table mostly derived from a table of  Laura Deming’s showing the greatest median lifespan increase that’s been achieved in mice by different therapeutic modalities.
   Note that median lifespan is not maximum lifespan, and lifespan often increased for only one sex of mouse.
 The top two modalities in mice, spontaneous mutation and GGT, can’t be used (legally) in humans.And I’d argue diet is off the table for practical reasons; we’re talking extreme diets.The remaining modalities are used in some form in humans.I left surgeries off the table since it’s too broad a category, but the ones relevant to humans give <= 10% median lifespan increase.Also note that just because a lab chose to treat a mouse with one modality doesn’t mean another modality can’t accomplish the same effect.Developing small molecule drugs to target proteins encoded by genes discovered by GGT in mice is standard practice.
 You can read this table as showing that human-amenable modalities are less potent than they could be, or you can read it as a selection effect of what academics find easiest to do in the lab.The former is a big claim, and I’d need more evidence to believe it.The latter isn’t news to anyone.Synthesizing candidate drug molecules is  slow.Producing immunotherapeutics is  slow and  hard.Gene therapy has a  ways to go.
 Altogether it seems clear that our therapeutic armamentarium is lacking, whether in potency or ease of use.
  Bringing a medical treatment to market is extremely slow and expensive.It  takes around a decade and $1 billion.A big reason for this is the  20% success rate for non-cancer drugs once they enter clinical trials, not to mention the definitely-less-than-100% success rate for making it to clinical trials in the first place.
 Here’s a figure from  this paper breaking down the costs, timelines, and success rates in a traditional target-based drug discovery campaign.
  Paraphrased caption from the paper:Numbers are in millions of USD and based on data from Eli Lilly and Company.p(TS) is the probability of successful transition from one stage to the next.WIP is work in process, the number of candidate drugs being worked on.Lighter-shaded boxes show calculated values based on assumed inputs.Costs do not include investments for exploratory discovery research, post-launch expenses like marketing, or non-R&D overheads like non-scientist salaries.
 Worth noting in the figure is that most of the cost is in the clinical trials (the blue parts), and that the step with the lowest success rate is Phase 2 clinical trials (the first time the therapy’s efficacy is tested in humans, usually).
 What does this mean for longevity?Barring an adventure in regulatory arbitrage or  medical tourism, any longevity treatments will have to get through this process before they can get to you or me.  3So it may well be that the best way to pursue longevity is to improve the drug development process, depending on how tractable and neglected various improvements are.
 Diagnosing the problems in the drug development process is a lot like studying aging: everything you look at is broken, but we have no idea about the root causes or where best to intervene.
  Animal models for disease are  fraught with  difficulty.Those for aging are  particularly  challenging.How challenging?So challenging that the longest-lived strain of mouse known to biology  used to be used as a model for  rapid aging, because the mice were being handled wrong and dying young.
 A big change in lab operations over the last two decades has been the “virtualization” of biotech, which is a fancy word for outsourcing.Work once done in-house, like synthesizing molecules or running toxicity assays, is now often done by Contract Research Organizations (CROs).Outsourcing carries all the advantages in biotech that it does in other domains: increased specialization, lower prices, etc.But outsourcing is inefficient at best, and usually involves many headaches.It’s still a lot easier to do drug development when everyone’s in the same building, or so says every ex-pharma scientist I’ve asked about it.This is not the case in other industries.
 Solutions to these problems could come in the form of automation —  Emerald Cloud Lab and  Transcriptic are two companies working on that — or better outsourcing offerings. One thing the field probably is good on is electronic lab notebooks; thanks though.
  Longevity research, and medicine in general, is bad at integrating its knowledge and learning from failures.Between 25% and 50% of clinical trial results  remain unpublished even several years after completion.This isn’t surprising: why publish your clinical data when there’s zero gain to you and even a slim chance you can repurpose the asset in the future?But this means a potential downside of moving longevity efforts from research to commercial therapeutic development is that the field will learn less overall.
 A related problem is that because liquidity events can happen far before convincing clinical data, biotechs are incentivized to push their risky studies until as late as possible (ideally after the employees get their money) and to gussy them up to look better than they really are.A fail-fast-and-publish-honestly mentality would of course be better for longevity overall.
 Then again, it’s not like academic research is  necessarily better.Medical research results in general are  apparently  unreproducible up to 50% of the time.Plus private biotech can sometimes afford to do things that academia can’t.
  Before it can conquer death, a longevity treatment will have to conquer the U.S. FDA’s clinical trial process.The FDA doesn’t consider aging to be a medical indication (a.k.a. a valid reason for treatment).This means longevity companies have to choose an existing age-related indication (e.g. Alzheimer’s) to demonstrate efficacy of their treatment on.How to do this well is a key consideration for any longevity biotech.
 More optimistically, regulatory changes like  dual-track clinical trials or  eliminating phase 3 trials altogether might entail a massive acceleration in longevity therapeutic development.Outsourcing or decentralizing clinical trials is also an exciting option. Science 37 is one company working on this.
  The high costs and failure rates of drug development make it a tough space for entrepreneurial disruption.(The first chapters  here are illustrative.)
 This is partly because high risk makes behavior change hard.There’s natural resistance to changing any part of the playbook.As one medicinal chemist put it to me “if you make a service 2x better, nobody cares.If you make it 10x better, or tell them to try a new approach, they won’t believe you. 4x or 5x better with their existing process is optimal.”Not exactly culture of disruptive innovation.Genetic sequencing has managed to jump this gap; I’d like to better understand what degree of price decrease and reliability was required for that.
 It’s also because the payoffs in biotech take so long to arrive, way after all the research is done.This means platforms and services for drug development aren’t bought and sold by biotechs as much as bartered for with equity in whatever future drugs they hopefully produce.This in turn makes it more complicated and expensive to start companies offering platforms and services to biotechs compared to other industries.
 As if all the above wasn’t good enough, the time and cost required to make a drug are increasing over time, despite lots of technological advancement.There’s even a cute name for it:  Eroom’s law.Nobody knows why it’s happening, but if the causes of Eroom’s law are exogenous, we should advance more longevity therapeutics sooner while it’s relatively cheap.
 So, which has more marginal benefit, improving the drug discovery system or going after longevity with the existing system?Obviously I have no idea, but the point is that it’s  not obviously the latter.We should also appreciate how many important problems there are to be solved outside the lab and with non-biology skills.
   The pursuit of longevity doesn’t happen in a vacuum.A full answer to “should we pursue human longevity” needs to take all externalities into account.
 A big externality is the aspirational value of longevity as a brand/meme for biotech in general.Some might argue that society is already working on longevity with every new medicine it develops.If we keep curing things that kill us, eventually we stop dying, right?Who needs the new branding?Well, a lot of longevity investment is not coming from traditional biotech/pharma investors, but from Silicon Valley.That may be attributable to the aspiration value.And even if the aspirational call of longevity does nothing more than encourage talented people to work on biotech, that may be well worth it.
 Another possibility is that the promise of death-postponement could spur funding increases or regulatory reforms for drug discovery.This is presumably the thinking behind efforts like  Rejuvenate,  Celevity, and  GenFlow to achieve longevity in charismatic species like household pets.If everyone’s dogs were suddenly living to age 30, people would hopefully start getting jealous.
 On the other hand, if a field over-promises and under-delivers it can go through a winter.This wasn’t great for the field of  AI, and it would be nice if longevity can avoid this.There’s also brand risk.Gene therapy was delayed a lot by the  death of a teenager in 1999.If longevity therapies develop brand recognition — I’m looking at you,  senolytics — and there’s a major adverse incident, it would have been better to keep a low profile.
 Then again, the same fears of death that make longevity prone to pseudoscience might act as a countervailing force to a winter. People don’t really care about AI research. They do care about staying alive.
   Why do we care about longevity? Because death is bad.  4 There are four problems with it:
  But longevity isn’t the only solution to these problems. And these problems aren’t the only causes of suffering in the world that we might care about. This means we have two opportunity cost calculations to make.
  Humanity has made a massive dent in problems 1 and 2 with analgesics.Analgesics get short shrift in the history of technology, but they’re one of the largest victories ever won against death, not to mention childbirth.They also have lots of room for improvement, though that improvement may be hard to attain if the history of analgesic development is  any  indication.Then again it’s hard to imagine it being any harder than longevity therapeutic development.
 Physical pain is hardly the only kind of suffering implicated in problems 1 and 2, of course.Aging and death, and witnessing them, entail  significant existential suffering and anguish.(Citation probably unnecessary for that fact.)If the psychonauts are to be believed, we may be close to treatments for those, too: clinical results so far are  promising.But it’s hard to imagine psychedelics making us fully disinterested in death.Doing so would likely require significant changes in analgesia, mood-altering drugs, and cultural attitudes toward medication and death.
 Some readers may feel that pain- or mood-manipulation are inauthentic ways to defy death.I’d gently remind such readers that we’re comparing these strategies against  making humans immortal, so you may want to check your naturalistic fallacy at the door.
 Problems 3 and 4 run up against hairy philosophical issue
......
title: 我们应该如何追求人类的长寿呢？
title_en: How should we pursue human longevity?

note:
胡说八道并不新鲜，但其数量之大，不断涌现的误导性故事、自相矛盾的叙述和彻头彻尾的虚假信息，已经改变了政治版图。多亏了数字技术，信息空间似乎比以往任何时候都更加失控。
出生于苏联的前真人秀制片人彼得·波梅兰采夫(Peter Pmerantsev)在2019年出版的一本书中声称，我们正在经历一种扭曲现实的政治，这种政治真正开始于后苏联时代的俄罗斯。波梅兰采夫出生于苏联，后来成为记者和学者。这是一种建立在一种独特的宣传形式上的政治，其目标是迷惑，而不是说服。
这也是非常愤世嫉俗的。波梅兰采夫说，重点不是兜售一种意识形态或对未来的愿景，而是告诉人们“真相是不可知的”，前进的唯一途径是“跟随一位强有力的领导人”。波梅兰采夫说，这种新风格并没有完全取代20世纪的旧宣传模式，但它正变得越来越普遍。
这本名为“这不是宣传”的书，是对旋转医生、政治操纵者和数字战略家世界的引人入胜的审视，这些人基本上都是在信息战的阴影下工作的人。波梅兰采夫的背景让他对现代政治马戏团有了一个独特的视角，而现代政治马戏团与真人秀节目越来越难以区分。
去年10月，在这本书出版后不久，我与波梅兰采夫进行了交谈，但随着选举的临近，这篇文章值得转发。我们的错误信息问题几乎肯定会变得更糟，而不是更好，这场关于数字宣传如何改变俄罗斯-以及现在的美国-政治的对话感觉更加相关。
首先，我想问你，你作为真人秀制片人的背景是如何影响你的政治态度的。
我可以肯定地说，它对我有帮助。自从电视问世以来，尤其是90年代以来，政客们的行为越来越像娱乐圈的人物，我们就处在这个奇怪的地方。在某种程度上，政治变得非常像另一个真人秀节目。
例如，在80年代，你有一个像里根这样的人，他是一名演员，扮演总统。但今天，像特朗普这样的人甚至不是在扮演总统-他只是一个扮演自己的演员。这是一件非常不同的事情，这是一个巨大的转变。
那么让我们来谈谈我们是如何走到这一步的。你的书从苏联解体后的俄罗斯和弗拉基米尔·普京开始。你争辩说，这种新的政治品牌，这种新的后现代宣传品牌，诞生于20世纪90年代和21世纪初的俄罗斯。
发生了几件事，他们从俄罗斯开始，然后转移到西方。首先，共产主义在90年代初崩溃，俄罗斯拥抱民主资本主义。但就在几年后，人们对民主和资本主义的信心开始消退。
因此，宣传人员正在协商一个新领域，在那里，伟大的想法不再重要。旧的社会身份已经崩溃，所有在苏联的旧职业和社会角色都已经崩溃。对未来已经没有概念了。政客们不是试图以理性的方式进行辩论，而是变成了这些伟大的表演艺术家，试图变得离谱，陶醉于他们不关心事实的事实。这一切都是非常新鲜的。
政治变得更多的是情感，而不是想法。你可以看到政客们在这段时间里改变了他们的做法。你可以看到这些像特朗普一样的人物使用与特朗普今天使用的类似的修辞策略，他们说的是完全荒谬的事情，你永远不会确定他们是认真的还是不是认真的。有时很好笑，有时很可怕……。
是的，这是一场表演，但是表演的元素非常有趣。20世纪的苏联政客撒了很多地狱般的谎言，但他们总是让自己的谎言听起来非常体面，就好像他们是真相一样。他们仍然在模仿事实话语，他们代表着一种重视证据语言的意识形态。
这些新的俄罗斯政客的不同之处在于，他们根本不玩这种事实游戏。他们不关心事实，也不假装关心。所以你不能真的揭穿他们的谎言，因为他们从来没有玩过这个游戏。这正是你现在看到的特朗普正在做的事情。
“我们现在生活在一种新的审查制度中，我们不了解我们周围的信息环境是如何形成的。”
我想澄清一下你在说什么：很多人仍然认为宣传是让谎言听起来真实的艺术，但这根本不是普京和特朗普这样的人想要的。他们想让真实成为一个无关紧要的类别。
这不是要证明什么，而是要怀疑。这一直是一种宣传功能，就像烟草公司试图让人们怀疑他们的产品是否会致癌一样。但总体而言，大多数政治意识形态并没有让人产生怀疑--他们声称说出了关于世界现在或应该是什么样子的真相。
但这一新的宣传是不同的。普京并不是在兜售一个美好的共产主义未来。他说，我们生活在一个黑暗的世界里，真相是不可知的，真相总是主观的，你永远不会知道它是什么，而你，这个小家伙，永远不会明白一切-所以你需要跟随一个强大的领导者。
这就是它现在的运作方式，只是它是从俄罗斯传播到世界各地的。
对共产主义或民主资本主义等宏伟意识形态的信仰崩溃是这个故事的一个重要部分。传统的政治范畴或身份在某个时候对人们来说不再有任何意义，这为…创造了这个新的空间。不管我们现在面对的是什么地狱。
当然，俄罗斯是最先到这一步的。宣传员和政客们找到了应对的策略。我记得几年前，在我读完上一本关于俄罗斯的书后，我在想：这是死胡同吗？还是这个怪胎显示了即将发生的事情的预兆？当然是这样的。
我几年前在俄罗斯看到的那种政治，就是我现在在英国、巴西、菲律宾和美国看到的那种政治。而互联网和数字媒体技术一直是这一切背后的重要工具。
在你的书中，你走遍了世界各地，与这场新的信息战前线的人们交谈-宣传员，宣传医生，社交媒体大师。这些人知道他们在做什么吗？他们在乎吗？他们只是虚无主义者吗？
这对我来说很有趣。这是我真的很想解决的事情。我与各种各样的人交谈-巨魔农场工人，数字战略家，帮助组织英国退欧的人。要在这个空间里工作，你必须是一个怪异的不道德的人。他们中的大多数人认为自己是政治艺术家。他们并不真正关心政策或想法。这只是一种游戏，他们能够将自己与后果分开。
乌克兰东部绝对是个疯子。从很多方面来说，这都是信息战的起点。那里的人们完全失去了现实感，以至于俄罗斯的炸弹袭击了乌克兰的平民领土，乌克兰人认为炸弹来自乌克兰。他们会对乌克兰士兵大喊大叫，士兵们会说，“不，不……。这是一枚炮弹，显然来自俄罗斯。“。但是没有人相信他们。
世界上所有的证据都不能说服他们。他们同情俄方，他们完全否认摆在他们面前的证据。他们重塑了世界，以符合俄罗斯宣传在他们头脑中植入的叙事。
谈论“另类事实”是一回事，但当你的房子被俄罗斯炮弹摧毁，而你还在胡说八道时，那是相当令人震惊的。
这种虚幻是如何被强化的呢？是智能手机吗？他们整天都在看俄罗斯国家电视台吗？
这主要是电视和谣言-这就是他们获取新闻的方式。在那里很难收看到乌克兰的电视节目。俄罗斯人已经在边境上安装了自己的信号塔，所以他们发射的信号要好得多。所以，即使你在乌克兰领土上，俄罗斯的信号也更强。然后分离主义组织运营的地方频道比乌克兰频道更强大。
你在书中对解决这个问题的可能性有点冷淡。这不是简单地调整系统或发明几项新技术的问题-数字时代的工具不能再放回盒子里，这让我们陷入了某种深渊。你有没有看到走出这场后现代噩梦的方法？
我尽量不要太听天由命，我真的不想给人留下我们没有办法的印象。毫无疑问，这其中有监管的部分。我们需要重新审视言论自由的原则，理解它们在数字时代的意义。
内容不再是战争的焦点所在。这是关于被操纵的竞选活动和互联网缺乏透明度的问题。我认为我们生活在一种新的审查形式中，我们不了解我们周围的信息环境是如何塑造的。我们不知道为什么算法只显示一段内容，而不显示另一段内容。我们不知道我们的数据中的哪些位被用来攻击我们，为什么。我们不知道我们看到的是有机的还是协同行动的一部分。这一切都是蒙在鼓里的。
我们需要揭开互联网后台的帷幕，这样我们才能看到信息是如何创造的，为什么是由谁创造的。而且，彻头彻尾的欺骗性活动应该是非法的。我这么说并不是说在互联网上撒谎的人应该入狱。但是，由巨魔农场、政府或私人公司组织的、明显具有欺骗性和伪装性的大规模活动应该是非法的。
最终，我们至少要认识到问题的严重性。两年前，人们还在告诉我，“这并不是什么新鲜事，人们总是说谎，政客们也总是说谎。”如果仍然这样想，那就是完全误解了技术是如何撕毁了旧的规则手册。
美国正在进行我们这个时代最重要的总统选举之一。至关重要的是，所有美国人都能够获得关于选举结果可能对他们的生活、他们的家庭和社区的生活意味着什么的清晰、简明的信息。这就是我们在Vox的任务。但是我们独特的解释性新闻需要资源。即使经济和新闻广告市场复苏，你们的支持也将是维持我们资源密集型工作的关键部分。如果您已经投稿了，谢谢您。如果你还没有，请考虑帮助每个人了解这次总统选举：今天只需3美元就可以捐款。
note_en:
Bullshit isn’t new, but the sheer volume of it, the constant flood of misleading stories, contradictory narratives, and outright disinformation, has changed the political landscape. The information space, thanks to digital technology, seems more out of control than ever.
 A 2019 book by Peter Pomerantsev, a Soviet-born ex-reality TV producer turned journalist and academic, claims that we’re experiencing a brand of reality-bending politics that really began in post-Soviet Russia. It’s a politics built on a distinctive form of propaganda, the goal of which is to confuse, not convince.
 It’s also exceptionally cynical. The point, Pomerantsev says, isn’t to sell an ideology or a vision of the future; it’s to tell people that “the truth is unknowable” and that the only way forward is “to follow a strong leader.” This new style has not entirely replaced the old 20th century model of propaganda, Pomerantsev says, but it’s becoming increasingly widespread.
 The book, titled   This is Not Propaganda, is a fascinating look into the world of spin doctors, political operatives, and digital strategists, basically all of the people working in the shadows of the information war. Pomerantsev’s background gives him a unique perspective into the modern political circus, which is increasingly indistinguishable from reality TV.
 I spoke with Pomerantsev last October, shortly after the book was released, but it’s worth reposting as we move closer to the election. Our misinformation problem is almost certainly  going to get worse, not better, and this conversation about how digital propaganda transformed Russian — and now American — politics feels all the more relevant.
   I want to start by asking you how your background as a reality TV producer informs your approach to politics.
  I can definitely say it has helped me. We’re in this weird place, really since the advent of TV and especially since the ’90s, where politicians are behaving more and more like entertainment figures. And at some point, politics became very much like another reality show.
 In the ’80s, for example, you had someone like Reagan, who was an actor playing the president. But today someone like Trump isn’t even playing a president — he’s just an actor playing himself. This is something very different, and it’s a big switch.
   So let’s talk about how we got to this point. Your book begins with post-Soviet Russia and Vladimir Putin. You argue that this new brand of politics, this new brand of postmodern propaganda, was born in Russia in the 1990s and 2000s.
   Several things happened, and they started in Russia before moving to the West. First communism collapses in the early ’90s and Russia embraces democratic capitalism. But just a few years later, faith in democracy and capitalism begins to fade.
 So propagandists are negotiating a new terrain where big ideas don’t matter anymore. Old social identities have collapsed, all the old professions and social roles that were in the Soviet Union have collapsed. There’s no idea of the future anymore. Instead of trying to argue in a rational way, politicians become these great performance artists, trying to be outrageous, reveling in the fact that they don’t care about the facts. And this is all very new.
 Politics becomes much more about feelings, not ideas. And you can see politicians changing their approach during this period. You can see these Trump-like figures who use similar rhetorical strategies that Trump uses today, where they say totally ridiculous things and you’re never quite sure if they’re serious or not. Sometimes it’s funny, sometimes it’s horrifying ...
    Right, it’s a show, but the elements of the show are very interesting. 20th-century Soviet politicians lied a hell of a lot, but they always made their lies sound very respectable, as if they were the truth. They were still imitating a factual discourse and they were representing an ideology that values the language of evidence.
 What was different about these new Russian politicians is that they just didn’t play this factuality game at all. They didn’t care about facts and didn’t pretend to care. So you couldn’t really call out their lies because they were never playing that game. And this is exactly what you see Trump doing right now.
 “We live in a new form of censorship now where we don’t understand how the information environment around us is shaped”
  I want to be clear about what you’re saying: A lot of people still think of propaganda as the art of making lies sound truthful, but that’s not at all what people like Putin and Trump are after. They want to make truthfulness an irrelevant category.
  It’s not about proving something, it’s about casting doubt. This has always been a function of propaganda, like the tobacco companies trying to make people doubt whether their product causes cancer. But overall most political ideologies have not been about casting doubt — they’ve claimed to be telling the truth about the way the world is or should be.
 But this new propaganda is different. Putin isn’t selling a wonderful communist future. He’s saying, we live in a dark world, the truth is unknowable, the truth is always subjective, you never know what it is, and you, the little guy, will never be able to make sense of it all — so you need to follow a strong leader.
 This is the way it works now, only it’s spread from Russia across the world.
  The collapse of faith in these grand ideologies like communism or democratic capitalism is a big part of this story. The traditional political categories or identities stopped meaning anything to people at some point and that created this new space for ... whatever the hell we’re dealing with now.
  For sure, and Russia just arrived at this point first. And the propagandists and the politicians found strategies to deal with it. I remember thinking a few years ago, after I finished  my last book about Russia: Is this the dead end? Or is this freak show a harbinger of things to come? And of course it was.
 The same sort of politics I saw in Russia years ago is the same kind of politics I’m seeing now in  the UK and  Brazil and  the Philippines and the US. And the internet and digital media technologies have been the essential tools behind all of it.
  For your book, you went around the world talking to people on the front lines of this new information war — the propagandists, the spin doctors, the social media gurus. Do these people know what they’re doing? Do they care? Are they just nihilists?
  This is very interesting to me. This is something I really wanted to work out. I talked to all kinds of people — troll farm workers, digital strategists, people who helped organized Brexit. To work in this space you have to be weirdly amoral. Most of them see themselves as political artists. They don’t really care about policies or ideas. It’s all just a kind of game and they’re able to dissociate themselves from the consequences.
     Eastern Ukraine is absolutely nuts. This is ground zero for the information war in a lot of ways. The people there have completely lost sense of reality, to the point where Russian bombs are hitting civilian territories in Ukraine, and the Ukrainians think the bombs are coming from Ukraine. They’ll scream at the Ukrainian soldiers, and the soldiers are like, “No, no, no, no ... it’s a shell, it obviously came from Russia.” But no one believes them.
 All the evidence in the world won’t persuade them otherwise. They were sympathetic to the Russian side, and they’ve completely disavowed the evidence in front of them. They’ve remade the world to fit the narrative planted in their heads by Russian propaganda.
 It’s one thing to talk about “alternative facts,” but when your actual house has been destroyed by a Russian shell, and you’ll still saying nonsense, that’s quite stunning.
  How is that unreality being reinforced? Is it smartphones? Are they watching Russian state TV all day?
  It’s mostly TV and rumors — that’s how they get their news. It’s very hard to get  Ukrainian TV there. The Russians have put their own signal towers on the border, and so they beam in with a much better signal. So, even though you’re in Ukrainian territory, the Russian signal is stronger. And then local channels run by separatist groups are stronger than the Ukrainian channels.
  You’re a little grim in the book about the possibilities of solving this problem. It’s not a matter of simply tweaking the system or inventing a few novel technologies — the tools of the digital age cannot be put back in the box and that leaves us in something like an abyss. Do you see any way out of this postmodern nightmare?
  I tried not to be too fatalistic, and I really don’t want to leave the impression that there aren’t steps we can take. There is, without a doubt, a regulatory part to this. We need to revisit the principles of freedom of expression and understand what they mean in the digital age.
 Content is not where the battle is anymore. It’s about manipulated campaigns and the lack of transparency of the internet. I think we live in a new form of censorship where we don’t understand how the information environment around us is shaped. We don’t know why an algorithm shows us one piece of content and not another. We don’t know which bits of our data are used to target us and why. We don’t know if what we’re seeing is organic or part of a coordinated campaign. All of this is in the dark.
 We need to rip off the curtain for the backstage of the internet so that we can see how information is created, and why, and by whom. And outright deceptive campaigns should be illegal. And by that I don’t mean that people telling lies on the internet should go to jail. But massive campaigns organized by troll farms or governments or private companies that are explicitly deceptive and camouflaged should be illegal.
 Ultimately, we have to at least recognize the scale of the problem. Two years ago people were still telling me, “Well this isn’t really new, people have always lied, politicians have always lied.” To still think this way to is to totally misunderstand how technology has ripped up the old rule book.
    The United States is in the middle of one of the most consequential presidential elections of our lifetimes. It’s essential that all Americans are able to access clear, concise information on what the outcome of the election could mean for their lives, and the lives of their families and communities. That is our mission at Vox. But our distinctive brand of explanatory journalism takes resources. Even when the economy and the news advertising market recovers, your support will be a critical part of sustaining our resource-intensive work. If you have already contributed, thank you. If you haven’t, please consider helping everyone understand this presidential election:   Contribute today from as little as $3.

note:
为了回应Facebook试图关闭纽约大学(New York University)的一个研究项目，该项目一直在收集社交媒体公司广告定向实践的数据，进步倡导团体共同事业发起了一场请愿活动，恳请这家科技巨头让该项目继续下去，取而代之的是关闭该平台上猖獗的虚假信息。
纽约大学广告观察站(NYU Ad Observatory)的研究人员招募了6500多名志愿者，通过使用专门设计的浏览器扩展来收集Facebook向他们展示的广告的数据。据“国会山报”上周晚些时候的报道，这家社交媒体巨头在10月中旬的一封信中通知纽约大学，该项目违反了网站禁止大量数据收集的规定。
此外，Facebook威胁称，如果该大学不终止研究，并在11月底之前删除其收集的数据，将采取额外的执法行动。
该项目的首席研究员劳拉·埃德尔森(Laura Edelson)解释说，我们开发了广告观察站(Ad Observatory)和广告观察者插件(Ad Viewer Plug-in)，以提供公众无法获得的网络安全分析的基本水平，并清楚地表明谁试图影响我们，以及为什么。
埃德森指出，考虑到当前选举周期中充斥着的争执和虚假信息，透明是必不可少的。她补充说，政治广告中的虚假信息和虚假信息是我们民主的网络安全漏洞。她补充说，透明是至关重要的，因为我们当前的选举周期中充斥着争执和虚假信息。她补充说，政治广告产生的错误信息和虚假信息是我们民主的一个网络安全漏洞。
麻省理工学院公民媒体中心(Center For Civic Media At MIT)主任伊桑·祖克曼(Ethan Zuckerman)将Facebook破坏纽约大学研究项目的努力描述为一种非常糟糕的形象。扎克曼表示，招募志愿者捐赠数据是改善社会的一种负责任的方式，让人们了解政治广告是如何定向的。
脸书的面貌非常糟糕-https://t.co/V0bPtXpRR7.。了解FB上的政治广告是如何定向的，这是公众和学术感兴趣的问题。招募志愿者捐赠数据是获得这些数据的一种负责任的方式。我们需要一个研究人员的避风港。
-Ethan Zuckerman(@Ethan Z)2020年10月23日
哥伦比亚大学骑士第一修正案研究所(Knight First Amendment Institute)诉讼主管亚历克斯·阿布多(Alex Abdo)表示，坦率地说，Facebook在大选前试图压制对政治虚假信息的研究，这令人震惊。现在没有什么问题比Facebook的决定如何塑造甚至扭曲政治话语更紧迫的问题了。
Abdo补充说，如果Facebook被允许成为有关Facebook的新闻和研究的看门人，这对民主来说将是可怕的。&#34；Abdo补充道，如果允许Facebook成为有关Facebook的新闻和研究的守门人，那将是可怕的。
共同事业的媒体与民主项目主任Yosef Getachew周一在一份声明中表示，美国人有权知道谁或什么试图影响我们在投票箱中的投票，并最终影响我们的政府。
Getachew指出，Facebook不遗余力地将自己描绘成民主的捍卫者，但在这里，它再次暴露出自己不愿履行任何可能损害其利润的承诺。
对我们民主的生死存亡的威胁。一场全球性的大流行。一场史无前例的经济危机。我们的新闻业从未像现在这样需要。
你今天能帮我们在11月2日前实现8万美元的秋季竞选目标吗？
Getachew补充说，尽管该公司承诺捍卫我们的民主，但Facebook现在正试图阻止纽约大学的公共利益研究人员调查该公司的广告定向和广告标签实践。
根据共同事业，纽约大学的研究人员对数千名志愿者在社交媒体上收到的广告进行了分析，发现Facebook在审查、正确标记谁为广告付费以及对其平台上的政治广告进行事实核查的承诺上存在重大失误。(译者注：根据共同事业组织的说法，纽约大学的研究人员对数千名志愿者在社交媒体上收到的广告进行了分析，揭示了Facebook在审查、正确标记谁为广告付费以及事实核查政治广告方面的重大失误。
在受到强大的社交媒体公司的威胁之前，研究人员发现，几个政治广告商违反了Facebook的披露规则，而不受惩罚。
Facebook上政治广告的披露违规行为普遍存在，这一披露将表明该平台未能遵守“诚实广告法案”(Honest Adds Act)的拟议要求。该法案得到了这家社交媒体公司的支持，该法案将要求数字平台明确说明谁为政治广告付费，并提供对联邦选举委员会(Federal Election Commission)强制执行违规行为的目标受众的描述。“诚实广告法案”是由这家社交媒体公司批准的，要求数字平台明确声明谁为政治广告付费，并提供对联邦选举委员会(Federal Election Commission)强制执行违规行为的目标受众的描述。
Facebook隐私政策官员艾莉森·亨德里克斯(Allison Hendrix)将该公司的广告库(其平台上的广告档案)描述为维护透明度和隐私的有效追踪工具。
但根据“共同事业”的说法，Facebook自己的广告库未能提供足够的透明度，其披露政策的执行不一致助长了选举虚假信息的传播。
“共同事业”负责活动的副总裁杰西·利特尔伍德(Jesse Littlewood)表示，Facebook现在要求纽约大学的研究人员停止他们的研究，而不是纠正自己记录在案的失败之处，履行自己的承诺。
利特尔伍德补充说，这是一种极其虚伪的行为。Facebook没有与其平台上猖獗的虚假信息和仇恨作斗争，而是决定追查那些帮助选民了解谁在试图影响他们的选票的人。
利特尔伍德解释说，共同事业组织了请愿书，要求Facebook首席执行官马克·扎克伯格和他的团队关闭他们平台上的虚假信息，而不是试图关闭试图揭露这些信息的倡导者和学者。
title: Watchdog反击Facebook试图关闭揭露广告实践中“政治虚假信息”的研究
title_en: Watchdog Fights Back as Facebook Attempts to Shut Down Research Exposing 'Political Disinformation' in Ad Practices

今年11月，不仅仅是文明的未来可供争夺。在这个移动社交计算的时代，我们正在弄清楚如何投票、娱乐、教学、学习，并致力于有意义的改变。由于大流行的紧急状态，我们改造我们国家和地球的计划立即被召回。
当前的政治动态很大程度上是通过“我们能负担得起多少改变？”这一观点来表达的。选举人团竞争中的摇摆州是那些受从化石燃料向可再生能源过渡影响最深的州。选择：从石油和天然气转向风能和太阳能，我们会失去多少工作岗位。宾夕法尼亚州、俄亥俄州、德克萨斯州和密歇根州的工人担心未来的再培训和中断会失去他们的生计。
无论我们坐在左翼还是右翼的哪个位置，我们都越来越明白，政府是不起作用的。竞选公职是对筹款的挑战，是你不能信守的承诺；立法是游说者的游乐场，特殊利益既不特殊，也不符合我们的利益。法院被时不时煽动和压制投票率的政治权力游戏搞得不堪重负。难怪对本周最后一场总统辩论的普遍反应是，竞选即将结束，这让人松了一口气。
对身体最重要的修正是静音按钮。在辩论中的短暂时刻，我们经历了几秒钟的沉默。时间似乎停滞不前，就像我们被递给了一块数字平板，上面写着不能做的事情：不要打断，不要不尊重，不要嘲笑，不要浪费我们的时间。最重要的是，不要忘记我们在病毒中失去的人。还记得那些日子吗？那时我们最大的问题是看什么节目，放什么音乐，讲什么笑话。当议程转回到我们自己的时候，你能听到什么是令人惊讶的。
在那一刻，你可以听到抚慰灵魂的声音。在那一刻，你可以听到领导人为了获得下一次投票而不得不说的话。不管这次选举结果如何，我对下次选举都感觉好多了。提前投票的爆炸性数字告诉我们很多关于这将如何进行的信息。精灵已经从瓶子里出来了，人们开始把这些点联系起来。如果投票被压制，只会让我们更加努力。
流动性是一种回归价值、回归根基、回归韧性的过程。在家工作是迈向随时随地生活的一大步。AR代表加速现实，VR代表有价值的现实。如果我们想知道社交有什么好处，打开静音按钮，听听你失去了什么。如果你可以将声音静音，你就可以取消静音并找到你的声音。
起初，静音按钮是一种防御性的举动。它与有线新闻网络的商业模式形成了反差，即重复填充党派观点，同时不听取对方的不满。我不得不做的最困难的事情就是对来自最不可能的地方的真相持开放态度。我们被教导要攻击对手的弱点；更好的策略可能是尊重他们的长处，并将其作为自己的长处。不过，别担心。你可能不会在那里找到太多可以反思的东西。
一旦你体验了静音按钮信封，你就可以听到它，即使它不在那里。修改后的辩论规则是，每个候选人回应的前两分钟都使用静音按钮，然后旧规则又回来了。即使在那时，使用静音按钮的经历也影响了剩下的辩论。特别值得注意的是，当主持人问乔·拜登是否有进一步的回应时，他对一系列反复无常的回答做出了回应。“…。…。…。没有。“
历史上还有其他静音按钮。当罗斯·玛丽·伍兹抹去了一盘至关重要的水门事件录像带时，18分半钟的间隙大声地说出了这一点。在此之前，我们假设可能有确凿证据。在那之后，我们知道可能还有其他人。在整个竞选过程中，我们可以通过倾听关键问题没有得到回答、回避或像皮维·赫尔曼(Pee Wee Herman)操场上的反驳那样被弹回对手的时刻，来更多地了解到底发生了什么。
很快我们就会知道这个重要问题的答案：我们如何对抗病毒？我投票赞成听科学，戴口罩，在线和线下都保持社交距离，快速测试，以及接触者追踪。以及同意的候选人。
吉尔莫团伙-弗兰克·拉迪斯、迈克尔·马克曼、基思·蒂尔、丹尼斯·庞布赖特、布伦特·利里和史蒂夫·吉尔莫。2020年10月23日星期五现场录制。
欲了解更多信息，请订阅吉尔莫帮派时事通讯，并在Telegram此处加入反向频道。
脸书…上的吉尔莫帮派。这是我们在Facebook上的姐妹秀G3。
note_en:
Not just the future of civilization is up for grabs this November. In this age of mobile social computing, we’re figuring out how to vote, entertain, teach, learn, and commit to meaningful change. Thanks to the pandemic emergency, our plans for transforming our country and planet are subject to immediate recall.
 Much of the current political dynamic is expressed through the lense of “how much change can we afford to make?” The swing states in the race for the electoral college are those most profoundly affected by the transition from fossil fuel to renewable energy. The choice: how many jobs will we lose by shifting away from oil and gas to wind and solar. Workers in Pennsylvania, Ohio, Texas, and Michigan are fearful of losing their livelihood to a future of retraining and disruption.
  Regardless of where we sit along the left/right spectrum, we share the increasing understanding that government doesn’t work. Running for office is a gauntlet of fundraising and promises you can’t keep; legislating is a lobbyist playground where special interests are neither special nor in our interests. The courts are overwhelmed by political power plays timed to inflame and suppress voting turnout. It’s no wonder that the common reaction to this week’s final presidential debate was relief that the campaign is almost over.
 The most important fix to the body politic is the mute button. For a brief moment in the debate, we got to experience a few seconds of not talking. Time seemed to stand still, as if we were being handed down a digital tablet of things to not do: don’t interrupt, don’t disrespect, don’t mock, don’t waste our time. Above all, don’t forget the people we’ve lost to the virus. Remember the days when our biggest problems were what show to watch, what music to play, what jokes to tell. It’s amazing what you can hear when the agenda is turned back to ourselves.
 In that moment, you can hear things that smooth the soul. In that moment, you can hear the words leaders will have to speak to get our vote next time. I feel much better about the next election no matter how this one turns out. The explosive numbers of early voting tell us a lot about how this will go. The genie is out of the bottle and people are beginning to connect the dots. If the vote is suppressed, it only makes us try harder.
 Mobility is about a return to value, to roots, to resilience. Working from home is a big step toward living from everywhere. AR stands for accelerated reality, VR for valued reality. If we want to know what social is good for, switch on the mute button and listen to what you’ve lost. If you can mute the sound, you can unmute it and find your voice.
 At first, the mute button was a defensive move. It counteracted the business model of the cable news networks, the repetitive time-filling of partisan perspective mixed with not listening to the grievances of the other side. The hardest thing I’ve had to do is be open to the truth emanating from the least likely location. We are taught to attack our opponent’s weaknesses; a better strategy might be to respect their strengths and adopt them as your own. Don’t worry, though. You probably won’t find too much there to reflect.
 Once you experience the mute button envelope, you can hear it even if it’s not there. The rules of the revised debate were that the first two minutes of each candidate’s response used the mute button, then the old rules returned. Even then, the experience of using the mute button informed the rest of the debate. Particularly noticeable was Joe Biden’s response to a series of back and forths when the moderator asked if he had any further response. “… … … No.”
 There have been other mute buttons in history. The 18 and a half minute gap spoke loudly when Rose Mary Woods erased a crucial Watergate tape. Before that, we assumed there might be a smoking gun. After that, we knew there might be others, too. Throughout the campaign, we could learn more about what was really going on by listening for the moments when key questions were left unanswered, ducked, or bounced back to the opponent like some Pee Wee Herman playground retort.
 Soon we’ll know the answer to the important question: how do we confront the virus? I vote for listening to the science, wearing a mask, socially distancing both off and online, rapid testing, and contact tracing. And the candidates who agree.
  The Gillmor Gang —  Frank Radice,    Michael Markman,    Keith Teare,    Denis Pombriant,    Brent Leary,   and  Steve Gillmor  . Recorded live Friday, October 23, 2020.
   For more,  subscribe to the Gillmor Gang Newsletter and join the backchannel  here on Telegram.

title: 吉尔莫帮派：不受压制的

向VR粉丝询问他们的抱怨，他们很可能会提到“屏蔽门”效果，或者当你看着离眼睛这么近的显示器时，你会注意到像素之间的差距。如果三星和斯坦福大学能如愿以偿，这种烦恼可能会完全消失。他们已经开发出(通过IEEE Spectrum)OLED技术，支持高达每英寸10,000像素的分辨率-远远高于你在几乎任何现有显示器上看到的分辨率，更不用说你在Oculus Quest 2这样的现代VR耳机中可以看到的分辨率了。
这项新的OLED技术使用薄膜在反射层之间发射白光，一个是银色的，另一个是由带有纳米波纹的反射金属制成的。这种“光学变形表面”改变了反射属性，并允许特定的颜色通过像素产生共振。该设计允许的像素密度比你在手机上看到的RGB OLED要高得多，但对亮度的影响不会达到你在一些电视上看到的白色OLED的程度。
这将是VR和AR的理想选择，可以创建一个几乎“无懈可击”的图像，你看不到屏蔽门的效果，甚至看不到单独的像素。这可能需要数年时间才能实现，届时需要更多的计算能力，但OLED技术将不再是障碍。
它也比你想象的更实用。三星已经在研究使用10000PPI技术的“全尺寸”显示屏，波纹的设计使大规模生产成为可能。这可能只是你何时何地看到这款OLED的问题，而不是“如果”的问题。
note_en:
Ask VR fans about their gripes and they’ll likely mention the “screen door” effect, or the gaps between pixels that you notice when looking at a display so close to your eyes. That annoyance might disappear entirely if Samsung and Stanford University have their way. They’ve  developed (via   IEEE Spectrum) OLED technology that supports resolutions up to 10,000 pixels per inch — well above what you see in virtually any existing display, let alone what you’d find in a modern VR headset like the  Oculus Quest 2.
 The newOLED tech uses films to emit white light between reflective layers, one silver and another made of reflective metal with nano-sized corrugations. This “optical metasurface” changes the reflective properties and allows specific colors to resonate through pixels. The design allows for much higher pixel densities than you see in the RGB OLEDs on phones, but doesn’t hurt brightness to the degree you see with white OLEDs in some TVs.
  This would be ideal for VR and AR, creating a virtually ‘flawless’ image where you can’t see the screen door effect or even individual pixels. This might take years to arrive when it would require much more computing power, but OLED tech would no longer be an obstacle.
 It’s also more practical than you might think. Samsung is already working on a “full-size” display using the 10,000PPI tech, and the design of the corrugations makes large-scale manufacturing viable. It may just be a question of when and where you see this OLED rather than “if.”
title: 三星，斯坦福制造10000 ppi的显示器，可能会带来“无懈可击”的虚拟现实
title_en: Samsung, Stanford Make a 10,000 PPI Display That Could Lead To 'Flawless' VR

福尔曼与石井浩教授一起在有形媒体研究小组工作，他于10月20日在计算机械协会用户界面软件与技术研讨会上介绍并展示了DefeXiles的研究。福尔曼表示，这种材料可能会立即被证明对时装设计中的原型和定制有用，但未来的应用还可能包括具有可调机械性能的3D打印外科网格等。
福尔曼说：“总的来说，这项工作最让我兴奋的是它对许多制作人的立竿见影的作用。”“与以前的工作不同的是，不需要定制的软件或硬件--只需要一台相对便宜的250美元的打印机，这是最常见的打印机类型--这真的让数百万人可以使用这项技术。”
“我们设想未来的材料将是动态的和可计算的，”石井说。“我们称之为‘基团原子’。”DefeXiles是自由基原子的一个很好的例子，它是一种可编程的物质，它模仿现有材料的属性，并超越了现有材料的属性。我们可以触摸、感觉、佩戴和打印它们。“。
与Forman和Ishii一起参与该项目的还有计算机科学和人工智能实验室、电气工程系和计算机科学研究生Mustafa Doga Dogan，以及麻省理工学院建筑系本科生汉密尔顿·福赛斯(Hamilton Forsythe)。
在麻省理工学院比特与原子中心主任尼尔·格申菲尔德教授的带领下，福尔曼在媒体艺术与科学课程MAS.863/4.140/6.943(如何制作(几乎)任何东西)上进行了3D打印实验。福尔曼的实验灵感来自他在卡内基梅隆大学读本科时的一位朋友的工作，他用未挤出的细丝制作花瓶。当他第一次尝试挤压不足时，我非常恼火，因为产生的瑕疵是完美的和周期性的，“他说，”但后来当我开始玩它，弯曲它，甚至拉伸它时，我就像是，‘哇，等等，这是一种纺织品。它看起来像它，感觉像它，弯曲像它，而且它打印得真的很快。“。
福尔曼补充道：“我带了一个小样本到我的班上做展示和讲解，没有多想，Gershenfeld教授看到了它，他对此很兴奋。”
当3D打印机未挤出材料时，它会在沉积的材料中产生周期性间隙。使用廉价的熔融沉积建模3D打印机，福尔曼开发了一种名为“球状拉伸”的欠挤出工艺，在这种工艺中，热塑性聚合物的球状颗粒通过细丝连接在一起。该工艺生产出一种柔软、有弹性的纺织品，具有明显的经纬度，就像机织织物一样。福尔曼说，它摸起来有点像网眼针织面料。
“这些纺织品不仅比其他方法更薄，印刷速度更快，而且演示表格的复杂性也得到了改善。福尔曼说：“使用这种方法，我们可以用普通的3D打印机打印3D壳体表格，而不需要特殊的切片机软件。“这很令人兴奋，因为3D打印面料有很多机会，但它真的很难传播，因为很多面料使用昂贵的机械和特殊软件或特殊命令，而这些通常是打印机特有的。”
这种新纺织品可以像熨烫的补丁一样缝制、去褶皱和热粘合。福尔曼和他的同事们使用许多常见的3D打印材料打印了这些纺织品，包括一种导电丝，他们用这种材料生产了一种灯，可以通过触摸灯罩上的褶皱来点亮和调暗。研究人员建议，其他基材或添加剂可以生产具有磁性或光学性能的纺织品，或者通过使用藻类、咖啡渣或木材生产更易生物降解的纺织品。
卡内基梅隆大学(Carnegie Mellon University)人机交互研究所(Human-Computer Interaction Institute)教授斯科特·哈德森(Scott Hudson)表示，福尔曼的工作代表着不断扩大的3D打印技术体系中一个非常有趣的补充。
“这项工作特别重要，因为它和更传统的技术一样，在相同的印刷过程中发挥作用，”哈德逊指出，他并没有参与这项研究。这项技术将使我们能够将定制的3D打印纺织品组件-可以是灵活和柔软的组件-与更传统的硬部件一起集成到物体中。
当麻省理工学院在新冠肺炎疫情爆发之初关闭时，福尔曼正在为美国医学会研讨会的提交做准备。他将自己设立的完整实验室搬到了纽约普莱西德湖附近父母小屋的地下室。
“这不是很多大型设备，但有很多小工具，钳子，细丝，”他解释说。“我不得不安装两台3D打印机，一个焊接站，一个照片背景--仅仅是因为这项工作涉及多个学科。”
福尔曼说，在机舱里，“当我周围的世界都在燃烧时，我能够专注于科学研究，这实际上是一种很好的分散注意力的方式。”“从事一个如此专注于科技的项目，然后向窗外看去，看到自然和树木-两者之间的紧张关系相当鼓舞人心，这也是一件有趣的事情。”
他回忆说，这对他的父母来说也是一次经历，他们看到了他“在我最紧张、最专注、最努力的时候”。“我会在早上5点上楼。我爸爸下来吃早饭的时候吃了点零食。
“我的父母成为创作活动的一部分，我会打印一些东西，然后去，‘看看这个，’”他说。“我不知道还会不会再有机会让我的父母如此近距离地触摸我每天做的事情。”
这个项目最不寻常的方面之一就是所谓的材料。福尔曼和他的同事们之所以使用“准纺织品”这个术语，是因为DefeXiles不具备通常纺织品的所有相同的物理特性，如两个方向的偏向和柔软度。但福尔曼说，一些怀疑者在感受到这些材料时已经皈依了。
这一经历让他想起了著名的勒内·马格里特(RenéMagritte)油画《物件的背叛(这不是烟斗)》，在油画中，烟斗的插图引发了一场关于一件作品是否能完全包含一个物件的所有意义的讨论。福尔曼解释说：“我感兴趣的是数字比特和材料体验之间的耦合，通过计算制造具有可控形式和机械性能的高保真材料。”
他补充说：“这让我想到，什么时候事物的指代成为人们所接受的事物。”“我感兴趣的不是人们的决定，而是它背后的道理，找出是什么导致他们接受或拒绝它作为纺织材料。”
note_en:
Forman, who works in the Tangible Media research group with Professor Hiroshi Ishii, presented and demonstrated the DefeXtiles research on Oct. 20 at the Association for Computing Machinery Symposium on User Interface Software and Technology. The material may prove immediately useful for prototyping and customizing in fashion design, Forman says, but future applications also could include 3D-printed surgical mesh with tunable mechanical properties, among other items.
 “In general, what excites me most about this work is how immediately useful it can be to many makers,” Forman says. “Unlike previous work, the fact that no custom software or hardware is needed — just a relatively cheap $250 printer, the most common type of printer used — really makes this technique accessible to millions of people.”
 “We envision that the materials of the future will be dynamic and computational,” says Ishii. “We call it ‘Radical Atoms.’ DefeXtiles is an excellent example of Radical Atoms, a programmable matter that emulates the properties of existing materials and goes beyond. We can touch, feel, wear, and print them.”
 Joining Forman and Ishii on the project are Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory and Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science graduate student Mustafa Doga Dogan, and Hamilton Forsythe, an MIT Department of Architecture undergraduate researcher.
  Forman had been experimenting with 3D printing during the media arts and sciences class MAS.863 / 4.140 / 6.943 (How to Make (Almost) Anything), led by Professor Neil Gershenfeld, director of the MIT Center for Bits and Atoms. Forman's experiments were inspired by the work of a friend from his undergraduate days at Carnegie Mellon University, who used under-extruded filament to produce vases. With his first attempts at under-extruding, "I was annoyed because the defects produced were perfect and periodic,” he says, “but then when I started playing with it, bending it and even stretching it, I was like, ‘whoa, wait, this is a textile. It looks like it, feels likes it, bends like it, and it prints really quickly.”
 “I brought a small sample to my class for show and tell, not really thinking much of it, and Professor Gershenfeld saw it and he was excited about it,” Forman adds.
 When a 3D printer under-extrudes material, it produces periodic gaps in the deposited material. Using an inexpensive fused deposition modeling 3D printer, Forman developed an under-extruding process called “glob-stretch,” where globs of thermoplastic polymer are connected by fine strands. The process produces a flexible, stretchy textile with an apparent warp and weft like a woven fabric. Forman says it feels something like a mesh jersey fabric.
 “Not only are these textiles thinner and faster to print than other approaches, but the complexity of demonstrated forms is also improved. With this approach we can print 3D dimensional shell forms with a normal 3D printer and no special slicer software,” says Forman. “This is exciting because there’s a lot of opportunities with 3D printing fabric, but it’s really hard for it to be easily disseminated, since a lot of it uses expensive machinery and special software or special commands that are generally specific to a printer.”
 The new textile can be sewn, de-pleated, and heat-bonded like an iron-on patch. Forman and his colleagues have printed the textiles using many common 3D printing materials, including a conductive filament that they used to produce a lamp that can be lit and dimmed by touching pleats in the lampshade. The researchers suggest that other base materials or additives could produce textiles with magnetic or optical properties, or textiles that are more biodegradable by using algae, coffee grounds, or wood.
 According to Scott Hudson, a professor at Carnegie Mellon University's Human-Computer Interaction Institute, Forman's work represents a very interesting addition to the expanding set of 3D-printing techniques.
 “This work is particularly important because it functions within the same print process as more conventional techniques,” notes Hudson, who was not part of the study. “This will allow us to integrate custom 3D-printed textile components — components that can be flexible and soft — into objects, along with more conventional hard parts.”
  When MIT closed down at the start of the Covid-19 pandemic, Forman was in the midst of preparing for the ACM symposium submission. He relocated his full lab set up to the basement of his parents’ cabin near Lake Placid, New York.
 “It’s not a lot of large equipment, but it’s lots of little tools, pliers, filaments,” he explains. “I had to set up two 3D printers, a soldering station, a photo backdrop — just because the work is so multidisciplinary.”
 At the cabin, “I was able to hone in and focus on the research while the world around me was on fire, and it was actually a really good distraction,” Forman says. “It was also interesting to be working on a project that was so tech-focused, and then look out the window and see nature and trees — the tension between the two was quite inspiring.”
 It was an experience for his parents as well, who got to see him “at my most intense and focused, and the hardest I’ve worked,” he recalls. “I’d be going upstairs at 5 a.m. for a snack when my dad was coming down for breakfast."
 “My parents became part of the act of creation, where I’d print something and go, ‘look at this,’” he says. “I don’t know if I’ll ever have the opportunity again to have my parents so closely touch what I do every day.”
 One of the more unusual aspects of the project has been what to call the material. Forman and his colleagues use the term “quasi-textile” because DefeXtiles doesn’t have all the same physical qualities of a usual textile, such as a bias in both directions and degree of softness. But some skeptics have been converted when they feel the material, Forman says.
 The experience reminds him of the famous René Magritte painting “The Treachery of Objects (This Is Not a Pipe),” where the illustration of a pipe prompts a discussion about whether a representation can fully encompass all of an object’s meanings. “I’m interested in the coupling between digital bits and the materials experience by computationally fabricating high-fidelity materials with controllable forms and mechanical properties,” Forman explains.
 “It makes me think about when the reference of the thing becomes accepted as the thing,” he adds. “It’s not the decision people make, but the reasoning behind it that interests me, and finding what causes them to accept it or reject it as a textile material.”
title: 利用3D打印机的“缺陷”来创造一种新的准纺织品
title_en: Leveraging a 3D printer “defect” to create a new quasi-textile

note:
伊斯兰堡(路透社)-巴基斯坦总理伊姆兰·汗(Imran Khan)呼吁Facebook FB.O在其平台上禁止仇视伊斯兰的内容，警告穆斯林中的激进化情绪激增。几个小时前，他抨击法国总统攻击伊斯兰教。
巴基斯坦周一召见了法国驻伊斯兰堡大使，因总统伊曼纽尔·马克龙对上周一名伊斯兰主义者杀害一名法语教师的反应引发的愤怒情绪蔓延。
可汗在周日发布在Twitter上的一封公开信中表示，“日益增长的伊斯兰恐惧症”正在鼓励全世界的极端主义和暴力，特别是通过Facebook等社交媒体平台。
汗说：“我会要求你们在Facebook上对伊斯兰恐惧症和仇恨伊斯兰教实施类似的禁令，就像你们为大屠杀实施的禁令一样。”
本月，Facebook表示正在更新其仇恨言论政策，以禁止否认或歪曲大屠杀的内容。
汗说：“人们不能发出这样的信息，即虽然针对一些人的仇恨信息是不可接受的，但这些信息对另一些人是可以接受的。”他补充说，这样的立场“反映了偏见和偏见，这将鼓励进一步的激进化”。
作为回应，Facebook的一位女发言人告诉路透社，该公司反对一切形式的仇恨，不允许基于种族、民族、国籍或宗教的攻击。
这位女发言人在一份声明中说：“一旦我们意识到这一点，我们将立即删除这种仇恨言论。”她补充说，Facebook有“更多的工作要做”。
Facebook上一份截至2019年12月的六个月透明度报告显示，巴基斯坦是限制内容请求数量第二高的国家，仅次于俄罗斯。
Facebook一位直接了解这一问题的消息人士告诉路透社，要求删除与伊斯兰教有关的内容的最多请求来自巴基斯坦，超过了其他穆斯林占多数的国家。
消息人士称，Facebook传统上遵守了这一规定，因为不这样做将违反巴基斯坦的法律，并补充说，当局经常会一次发送数十个链接，并要求删除这些链接。
在信中，汗还提到了法国的情况，他说，在法国，伊斯兰教与恐怖主义联系在一起。
汗说，马克龙通过鼓励展示描绘先知穆罕默德的漫画来“攻击伊斯兰教”。
汗发表评论之前，马克龙向一名被一名伊斯兰激进分子斩首的法国历史教师致敬，这名教师想要报复在一堂关于言论自由的课上使用描绘先知穆罕默德的漫画。
巴基斯坦外长沙阿·马哈茂德·库雷希表示，法国驻伊斯兰堡大使已被召见，对马克龙的“不负责任的言论”提出外交抗议。
土耳其总统塔伊普·埃尔多安(Tayyip Erdogan)也批评了马尔孔，称上周法国总统在对待穆斯林的态度上需要心理帮助。法国随后召回了驻土耳其大使。
巴基斯坦和土耳其有着牢固的关系，而汗和埃尔多安则发展了密切的关系-特别是在伊斯兰恐惧症的话题上。
汗写给脸书的信和对马克龙的评论出现在他面临新成立的反对党联盟试图推翻他的压力之际。
丹麦报纸Jylland-Posten于2005年首次出版了先知穆罕默德的漫画，长期以来，这些漫画在穆斯林中一直是一个敏感话题，在巴基斯坦和其他地方引发了抗议活动。
上个月，当法国讽刺周刊查理周刊转载这些漫画时，数万人在巴基斯坦举行了抗议活动。
汗政府已经对一些社交媒体应用程序进行了严格的审查，并禁止了一些被当局视为淫秽或不道德的内容。

ISLAMABAD (Reuters) - Pakistan Prime Minister Imran Khan called on Facebook   FB.O  to ban Islamophobic content on its platform, warning of a spike in radicalisation amongst Muslims, hours after he hit out at the French president for "attacking Islam".
  Pakistan summoned the French ambassador in Islamabad as anger spread on Monday over President Emmanuel Macron’s reaction to the murder last week of a French teacher by an Islamist.
 Khan, in an open letter posted on Twitter on Sunday, said “growing Islamophobia” was encouraging extremism and violence worldwide, especially through social media platforms such as Facebook.
 “I would ask you to place a similar ban on Islamophobia and hate against Islam for Facebook that you have put in place for the Holocaust,” Khan said.
  This month, Facebook said it was updating its hate speech policy to ban content that denied or distorted the Holocaust.
 “One cannot send a message that while hate messages against some are unacceptable, these are acceptable against others,” Khan said, adding such a stance was “reflective of prejudice and bias that will encourage further radicalisation”.
 In response, a Facebook spokeswoman told Reuters the company opposed all forms of hate and did not allow attacks based on race, ethnicity, national origin or religion.
  “We’ll remove this hate speech as soon as we become aware of it,” the spokeswoman said in a statement, adding that Facebook had “more work to do”.
 Facebook’s last transparency report for the six months to December 2019, showed that Pakistan was the source of the second highest number of requests to curb content after Russia.
 A source with direct knowledge of the issue at Facebook told Reuters that the most requests for the removal of content relating to Islam came from Pakistan, exceeding other Muslim-majority countries.
  Facebook traditionally complies, as not doing so would be to break Pakistan’s laws, the source said, adding that authorities would often send dozens of links at a time and demand they be taken down.
  In his letter, Khan also referred to the situation in France, where, he said, Islam was being associated with terrorism.
  Khan said Macron had “attacked Islam” by encouraging the display of cartoons depicting the Prophet Mohammad.
 Khan’s comments came after Macron paid tribute to a French history teacher beheaded by an Islamist radical who wanted to avenge the use of cartoons depicting the Prophet Mohammad in a class on freedom of expression.
 Pakistani Foreign Minister Shah Mehmood Qureshi said the French ambassador in Islamabad had been summoned to be given a diplomatic protest against Macron’s “irresponsible remarks”.
  Turkish President Tayyip Erdogan also criticised Marcon, saying last week the French president needed mental help over his attitude towards Muslims. France then recalled its ambassador to Turkey.
 Pakistan and Turkey share strong ties, while Khan and Erdogan have developed a close relationship - particularly on the topic of Islamophobia.
 Khan’s letter to Facebook and comments on Macron come as he faces pressure from a newly formed coalition of opposition parties seeking to oust him.
  The cartoons of the Prophet Mohammad, first published in 2005 by Danish newspaper Jyllands-Posten, have long been a sensitive topic among Muslims, stirring protests in Pakistan and elsewhere.
 Tens of thousands of people protested in Pakistan last month when the cartoons were republished by the French satirical weekly Charlie Hebdo.
 Khan’s government has subjected a number of social media apps to intense scrutiny and banned some for content authorities deemed obscene or immoral.
title: 巴基斯坦总理要求Facebook禁止仇视伊斯兰的内容
title_en: Pakistan's PM Asks Facebook To Ban Islamophobic Content
